title: The Universal Grammar
subtitle: [count] [register] operator [count] motion
summary: Vim is a tiny language: verbs (operators) compose with nouns (motions and text objects). Once you see the grammar, every key falls into place.

blocks.text: Almost every command in Vim follows one shape:

Read it as a sentence: *do this thing*, optionally *to register r*, *this many times*, *to this stretch of text*. (Registers are named clipboards — the `"a` prefix means "into register `a`"; the full treatment is in Part 12.) Concrete examples:

rows: Delete three words forward (counts multiply, but here one of them is 1)
Change the inner word the cursor is on
Yank from cursor to end of line

blocks.text: Anything that moves the cursor in *normal mode* is a motion. After an operator, the same motion describes the *range* the operator acts on. So {key:w} alone moves the cursor a word forward; {key:d}{key:w} deletes the same span.

blocks.title: Why It Composes
blocks.text:
Operators and motions live behind a clean interface. A motion is a function `cursor → (start, end, type)`, where *type* is `char`, `line`, or `block`. A text object is a function `cursor → (start, end, type)` that doesn't have to use the cursor as one of the endpoints. An operator is a function `(start, end, type) → effect-on-buffer`.

This is why every operator works with every motion and every text object. Vim's source code does not have a giant switch statement of "if d, then handle w specially." It has *one* operator dispatch and *one* motion dispatch, and they meet through the `(start, end, type)` triple. When you learn a new operator, you've learned its combination with every motion you already know. When you learn a new text object, you've learned its combination with every operator. The grammar scales multiplicatively.

blocks.title: The Doubled Form
blocks.text: Every operator has a *doubled* form that means "apply linewise to this line": {key:d}{key:d} deletes the line, {key:y}{key:y} yanks the line, {key:c}{key:c} changes the line, {key:>}{key:>} indents the line, {key:g}{key:U}{key:U} uppercases the line. This is a syntactic shortcut — internally, {key:d}{key:d} is processed as a special case where the motion is replaced by "the current line, linewise."

blocks.title: The Wart: {key:Y}, {key:D}, {key:C}, {key:S}
blocks.text: Vi shipped before the doubled-form rule was systematic. {key:Y} is *not* {key:y}{key:$} — historically it was {key:y}{key:y} (yank line). Modern Neovim has finally fixed this default, but vanilla Vim still aliases {key:Y} to {key:y}{key:y}. {key:D}, on the other hand, *is* {key:d}{key:$} — it has always been the consistent form. {key:C} is {key:c}{key:$}. {key:S} is {key:c}{key:c}. {key:s} is {key:c}{key:l}. These shortcuts predate the unified grammar and are kept for muscle memory. Treat them as exceptions to learn, not principles to derive.

When you want to learn a new key, ask: is it a *verb* (operator), a *noun* (motion / text object), or a *complete command* (like {key:x} or {key:p})? If it's the first two, you've immediately doubled the size of your vocabulary by combining it with everything you already know.
